name: Russia
summary: Day 50. Russian oil sanctions waiver expired April 11. During the 30-day waiver window, Indian refiners ordered ~30-60M barrels (figures vary: India Weekly ~30M; gg2.net 30M; some sources 60M including pre-waiver commitments). This Russian buffer is India's primary crude reserve as Iran waiver also expires Sunday April 19. India's 48-hour window to full-sanctions squeeze: both Russian and Iranian sanctioned doors slam shut while Hormuz remains port-blockaded. Russia's structural play unchanged: satellite intelligence sharing with Iran, MT Arhimeda VLCC testing inbound Hormuz, UNSC veto blocking Hormuz resolution. Bifurcated position persists: support Iran diplomatically, benefit from elevated oil prices, position for OPEC+ post-war supply increase. Russia benefits from both sides of the India recalibration — as the waiver-window supplier now, as the post-war OPEC+ revenue beneficiary later.

signals.content: Russia and China raised diplomatic voices against US-Israeli attacks. Coordinated emergency UNSC sessions. Condemned as sovereignty violations.
Russia providing intelligence to Iran including data on US military positions. Logistical support. But no direct military intervention.

Russia remains an intelligence/diplomatic supporter of Iran. Pravda reported Russia 'poised to ease global helium crunch' (March 30) but no confirmed supply deals have materialized as of Day 38. Urals crude at $123.45 — Russian benchmark surging alongside global crude. Russia's OPEC+ participation in the 206K bpd increase is nominal. Russia's UN Security Council veto power blocks any UNSC resolution on the conflict. No direct military involvement but strategic posture unchanged.

Russia friendly-flag in Iran's three-tier Hormuz system per Bloomberg Tracker — vessels flagged to Russia continue to transit. Russia voted with OPEC+ for the April 5 206K bpd May increase (designed to flood markets the moment Hormuz reopens). Russia is structurally aligned with Iran on shielding the resistance axis but quietly aligned with Saudi/UAE on post-war pumping. The bifurcation lets Russia profit either way: war = high oil prices for Russian exports; peace = aggressive market share post-reopening.

Growing market concerns over potential US tariffs on Russian palladium (~40% of global supply) drove palladium +8.2% to $1,560 on April 8 — the largest PGM move. EFP (exchange for physical) activity spiking. Only ~20% of palladium's move is ceasefire-linked; Russian tariff story is the dominant driver and persists regardless of Middle East outcomes. This is a SEPARATE geopolitical risk from the West Asia crisis. (Source: market data, industry analysis)

Russia and China vetoed Bahrain's UNSC resolution to keep Hormuz open (April 7, 11-2 vote, France abstained). Resolution originally invoked Chapter VII (force authorization), watered down but still vetoed. Russia's rationale: resolution 'unbalanced, confrontational' for focusing solely on Iran. China: 'failed to capture root causes in balanced manner.' The veto closes the multilateral forced-reopening pathway — any Hormuz resolution now goes through bilateral Islamabad talks (where Russia has no seat) or unilateral IRGC toll (which benefits Russia via higher oil prices). Russia's bifurcated position: support Iran diplomatically at UNSC while benefiting from elevated oil prices funding its own war effort, AND positioning for OPEC+ post-war supply increase. (Source: PBS, Al Jazeera, Shafaq News, UN News, The Hill)

Ukrainian intelligence reports Russian satellites conducted at least 24 detailed imagery surveys of 46 military and critical sites across 11 Middle Eastern countries from March 21-31, and shared imagery with Iran through a 'permanent communications channel.' Imagery covers US and allied military bases, airports, and oil fields. Russian satellites 'actively surveying' Strait of Hormuz, which would help Iran enforce blockade more effectively and track foreign naval movements. Russian and Iranian hackers reportedly collaborating in cyber domain. Single intelligence source (Ukraine) with own interest in framing Russia negatively, BUT specificity of claim (24 surveys, 46 objects, 11 countries) lends credibility, and Russia-Iran military cooperation well-documented pre-war. (Sources: Reuters via US News April 7, Military Times, Times of Israel, Modern Diplomacy — all citing Ukrainian intelligence)

Day 44. RUSSIAN VLCC TESTING INBOUND HORMUZ PASSAGE — MT ARHIMEDA HEADED TO KHARG ISLAND [CONFIRMED via AIS]. A Russian-flagged VLCC, MT Arhimeda (previously US-sanctioned under the former name 'Vizuri'), transited INTO the Gulf April 9-10 heading toward Iran's Kharg Island. This is significant for three reasons: (1) Russia is one of only four nations operating Russian-flag VLCCs — this is not a shadow-fleet minor player, it's a flag-state action. (2) The direction is INBOUND, not outbound — Russia is testing whether it can lift Iranian crude directly under the IRGC tiered toll regime, not just receive delivery elsewhere. (3) The vessel was previously US-sanctioned, so the transit is a deliberate statement about sanctions enforcement under the ceasefire. Russia is in the IRGC's 'allies' pricing tier at $0.50-0.70/bbl alongside China, with payment via yuan/CIPS/Bitcoin/USDT — entirely outside SWIFT. Russia's structural play: whether Islamabad succeeds or fails, Moscow gains optionality on discounted Iranian crude at the lowest tier of the IRGC toll. If the ceasefire holds, Russia has a preferential lane; if it fails, Russia benefits from higher global oil prices. Either outcome is net positive for Russian energy revenue. For the russia→iran edge: context should note this is now material energy cooperation, not just intelligence/diplomatic support. (Sources: gCaptain, Bloomberg AIS, Windward AI, Lloyd's List)

Day 48. US SANCTIONS WAIVER FOR RUSSIAN OIL PURCHASES BY INDIA EXPIRED APRIL 11 [CONFIRMED]. CNBC reported this as 'potentially the most significant India-specific development of the week' — the waiver expired BEFORE the Hormuz blockade even took effect on April 13, meaning India can no longer buy Russian crude (its largest alternative source since 2022) without risking secondary US sanctions. India had been importing approximately 1.5-2M bpd from Russia under the waiver arrangement. The expiry compounds India's triple squeeze: (1) Russian waiver expired April 11, (2) Iran waiver expires April 19 (4 days away), (3) physical Hormuz blockade blocks remaining supply routes. Russia's role in the Indian energy market shifts from lifeline to sanctioned supplier. For Russia itself, the waiver expiry reduces demand for Russian Urals crude from its second-largest buyer — but with global oil prices elevated, the revenue impact is partially offset by higher per-barrel pricing to remaining customers (China, Turkey). The Atlantic Council confirmed the waiver details. This is the first time since India began heavy Russian crude purchases in 2022 that the sanctions pathway has been formally closed.

edges.context: Providing US military position data. Diplomatic backing at UNSC. UNSC veto blocks any resolution. Satellite intelligence sharing: 24 surveys of 46 sites in 11 countries shared via permanent communications channel (Ukrainian intel, April 14). Active Hormuz surveillance supporting blockade enforcement. But no troops or weapons. Strategic caution.
Joint UNSC actions. Both condemning strikes. Both providing limited support but no military involvement.
Russia benefits from high oil prices as major energy exporter. Urals at $123.45. War increasing Russian oil revenues despite sanctions.
Russia reported 'poised to ease global helium crunch' (Pravda March 30) but no confirmed deals as of April 6. If materialized, could partially offset Qatar's 30-38% offline.
